                                                           10
Recap: Statements
An instruction that the Python interpreter can execute.

Assignment                     x = 5
Conditional statements         if | else | elif
Error handling                 try | except
Loops                          for, while
   – Control statements        break, continue
Function definitions           def
                            11
Today
• Conditional Statements

• Exception Handling

• Loops

• Function
                                                                             12
Conditional Statements
• Conditional statements
   ⎻ allow different blocks of code to be executed depending on whether a
     given condition is True or False
   ⎻ create branches in the flow of a program

• Branching allows us to run different statements for different
 conditions.
• The simplest branching is the if statement.
                                                 13
   Elements of an if statement
                 2. A condition
                 (must result in boolean)
1. The word if

         if x > 0:   3. A colon (:)
              print('x is positive')

                 4. An indented block of code
                                                                14
Meet the Condition?!
• If the condition is True, indented statements are executed
• If the condition is False, indented statements are skipped


  x = 10
  if x == 5:
       print('x is equal to 5') # indented
       print('This line will also be printed if x == 5')


  print('This line will be always printed')
                                                                               15
Relational Operators Review

Relational operators compare     We leverage these relational operations
operands and produce a           to define conditions
Boolean                          x = 10
                                 if x > 5:
• equal: ==                          print('x is greater than 5')
• not equal: !=
• greater than: >                if x < 10:
                                     print('x is less than 10')
• less than: <
• greater than or equal to: >=   if x <= 10:
• less than or equal to: <=          print('x is less than or equal to 10')
                                              16
Exercise
Which of the following is correct?

     (a) if x == 5:
           print('x is equal to 5')

     (b) if 'x == 5':
           print('x is equal to 5')

     (c) if 'x = 5'
           print('x is equal to 5')

     (d) if x = 5:
           print('x is equal to 5')

                                      CODE
                                                                       17
Two-option Decision
• What if we want to take different actions, depending on whether a
 condition is True or False?
  ⎻ One action if salary is > $50,000, one if not
  ⎻ One action if grade is above 75, one if not
  ⎻ One action if the user input is valid, one if not

• In Python, done with if… else…
      month = 10
      if month == 5:   # Option 1
          print('It is May')
      else:            # Option 2
          print('It is not May')
                                                                         18
Example for Two-option Decision
 if x % 2 == 0:          • If remainder of x divided by 2 is 0, we
    print('x is even')     know x is even. The program displays a
 else:                     message to that effect
    print('x is odd')
                         • If the condition is false, the second set
                           of statements is executed

                         • Since the condition must either be True
                           or False, exactly one of the
                           alternatives will be executed




                                                                 CODE
 Multiple-option Decision: elif          19

elif is short for "else if"

       score = 85
       if score >= 90:
           grade = 'A'
       elif score >= 80:
           grade = 'B'
       elif score >= 70:
           grade = 'C'
       else:
           grade = 'D'
       print(grade)

                                 CODE
                                                                        20
Logical Operators in Conditions
Recall that Logical Operators can be applied to conditions
• The and statement is only True when all conditions are True
• The or statement is True if at least one condition is True
• The not statement outputs the opposite truth value
      a = 10
      b = 10
      c = -10
      if a > 0 and b > 0 and c > 0:
          print("a, b and c are greater than 0")
      else:
          print("At least one number is not greater than 0")

                       What if we change and to or?
                                                                CODE
                            21
Today
• Conditional Statements

• Exception Handling

• Loops

• Functions
                                                                                          22
Except Statement for Error Handling
• Exception statement try … except … : a conditional execution structure to
  handle errors (called "exceptions")
• Exceptions handled gracefully, rather than letting program terminate abruptly
• How it works:
   ⎻ If code in try block generates no exceptions, except block is skipped
   ⎻ If exception occurs within try block, rest of try block is skipped, and
     statements in except block are executed

                  try:
                      val = 'a' + 5
                      print('val = ', val)
                  except:
                      val = -100
                      print('an error occurred')
                                                                                  CODE
 Exception Statement with Multiple                                              23

‘except’
A try statement may have more than one except clause, to specify
different handlers
• At most one handler will be executed
• The last except clause may omit exception names, to serve as a wildcard to
 catch all unexpected errors that were not handled

try:
       # code that might trigger an exception
except Exception1:
       # execute if there’s an Exception1
except Exception2:
       # execute if there’s an Exception2
except:
       # execute for all other exception types
                                                                           24
   Example for Multiple ‘except’ Clauses
# At most one handler will be executed, just like if-elif-else

try:
    y = int(input("Enter a number: "))
    y = 1 / y
    print("the rest of the code in try clause")
except ValueError:
    print("ValueError: input() did not work. Please enter an integer.")
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print("ZeroDivisionError: do not divide by 0! ")
except: #for all other error types
    print("unexpected error")

print("this code always runs")


                                                                   CODE
                            25
Today
• Conditional Statements

• Exception Handling

• Loops

• Functions
                                                                26
What is a Loop?
• A segment of code that repeatedly executes a block of code
                  i = 0
                  while i < 5:
                      print(i)
                      i = 1 + 1
                                                                                 27
Two Types of Loops in Python
• for
  ⎻ iterates over a sequence (list, tuple, string) or other iterable objects.


• while
  ⎻ repeatedly execute a block of code for as long as the condition is
    True
                                                                    28
  Elements of a for statement

1. The word for      2. An interator
                                                  3. A colon (:)
   for techCo in ['nVidia', 'Apple', 'Meta']:
         # this line has an indent
         print(techCo + 'begins with ', techCo[0])
         print(techCo + 'has' + str(len(techCo)) + 'characters')
                   4. An indented block of code
   print('Finished looping')
                                                                29
for Loop
• Used to iterate over a sequence of elements
• Executes the indented code block once per element

  # dayOfWeek is a list
  dayOfWeek = ["Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday"]

  for d in dayOfWeek: #iterate the Elements in a List
      print(d)



                                                        CODE
                                                               30
Iterate Elements in range()
• range() function generates a sequence of numbers that can
 be used in a for loop

range(3)                     range(3, 9)
                             excludes 9
                                                                                         31
Types of for Loop Iteration
Iteration by index                          Iteration by element

for i in range(len(alist)):                 for elem in alist:
    alist[i] = alist[i] * 10                    elem = elem * 10
print(alist)                                print(alist)

• Loop variable i is the increasing index   • Loop variable elem takes the value of
  number to access items in the list by       each element in alist directly, NOT the
  putting index in square brackets, [i]       index
• Iteration by index allows read access     • Iteration by element allows read access
  and modification of the individual          to the items in a sequence
  items                                     • This does NOT allow modification of
                                              the individual items, because
                                              replacement of elements by item
                                              assignment requires an index number
 Exercise                                                                            32

numbers = [5, 11, 7, 13, 9, 17]
total = 0
# note: sum is a built-in function; do not use as variable name

for num in numbers: # iterate over the list
    total = total + num # or total += num
    print ("The sum is", total)
                                                  What is the printout?
                num         total
                                                   The sum is 5
1st iteration   5           5+0=5                  The sum is 16
2nd iteration   11          5 + 11 = 16            The sum is 23
                                                   The sum is 36
3rd iteration   7           16 + 7 = 23            The sum is 45
…                                                  The sum is 62


Question: This is iteration by element. Would iteration by index be more suited?
                                                                             CODE
                                                                33
 while Loop
• Repeats a code block based on a condition
• Execution of code block will occur until the given logical
 condition is False
• The number of iterations is unspecified
                                                                     34
    Elements of a while statement

1. The word while 2. A boolean expression
     techCo = ['nVidia', 'Apple', 'Meta']
     i = 0
     while i < len(techCo):     3. A colon (:)
         # this line is indented
         print(techCo[i] + 'begins with ', techCo[i][0])
         print(techCo[i] + 'has' + str(techCo[i]) + 'characters')
         i += 1 # equivalent to i = i + 1

             4. An indented block of code
                                                                      35
  while loop examples


i = 0          i = 0          i = 1           i = 0
while i < 5:   while i < 5:   while i <= 5:   while i <= 5:
    print(i)       print(i)       print(i)        print(i)
    i += 1                        i += 1          i += 1




                                                              CODE
                                                           36
break and continue Statements

• Alter the normal flow of control in a loop
• Help avoid deeply nested loops
• Can make code more readable and easier to maintain
• break — jumps immediately OUT of the loop
• continue — jumps immediately to NEXT ITERATION of the
 loop
                                                                          37
for/while loop with break
break immediately terminates (exits) the loop

      years = [2000, 2001, 2003, 2012]
      i = 0
      year = years[0]

      while(year != 2012):                      What is the printout?
          print(year)
          i = i + 1
          if year == 2001:
              break
          year = years[i]


                                                                  CODE
                                                                       38
for/while loop with continue
• continue immediately jumps to next loop iteration (without
 completing the current one)

     years = [2000, 2001, 2002] # list of three numbers
     length = len(years) # here length = 3
     for i in range(length):
          if years[i] == 2001:
               continue
          print(years[i])
                                     What is the printout?


                                                               CODE
 39
                            40
Today
• Conditional Statements

• Exception Handling

• Loops

• Functions
                                                       41
What is a Function?
• Reusable code that performs a specific task
• Once defined, may be called anywhere in your code
• Two types
  ⎻Built-in, e.g., print(), int(), len()
  ⎻User-defined
                                                           42
Why create user-defined functions?
• Provide for reusable code
• Clarify code logic by breaking it into discreet steps
                                                                            43
 Elements of a Function Definition
           2. Your function name
            (same rules as for variables:
            letter, digits, underscore)
                                        3. Parameter list in parentheses
1. The word def
                  def myAddition():
                      x = 5              4. A colon (:)
                      y = 10
                      print(x + y)


                          5. Indented code block
                                                          44
User-defined Functions
• Once defined, a function can be reused over and over
# create a function myAddition()
def myAddition():
    x = 5
    y = 10
    print(x + y)

print("Hello")
myAddition() # call the function
myAddition() # call the function
                                                                    45
Function example: type()

           • Function name is type
           • Expression(s) in parentheses (32) are called
            argument

           • Arguments are values passed as input to the
            function

           • Functions can return a value as a result
              ⎻ type returns the data type of its argument (32 =
                int)
                                                                       46
Function Argument
Arguments:
     values passed to a function for the function to do things with

print(10) # 10 within the parentheses is an argument


                         arguments

len("this is a string")
                                                                        47
Functions can accept arguments
We can define a function with parameters (essentially variables) and
accept arguments
                                   Arguments are "copied" to
 def myAddition2(x,y):             parameters in function:
       print(x + y)                       x=5
                                          y=3
 myAddition2(5,3)                  Parameters exist only within the
                                   current call to the function
                                                               48
Exercise — add error checking

# create a function called myAddition2
def myAddition2(x,y):
    print(x + y)


myAddition2(1, 2)          # works
myAddition2(2.5,(2.3+3.9)) # works
myAddition2(3, "4")        # should generate error
myAddition2(2,(3==5))      # works

                               What is the printout?   CODE
                                                             49
Exercise — add error checking
# create a function called myAddition3
def myAddition3(x,y):
     try:
          print(x + y)
     except:
          print("myAddition2 expects two numbers")

myAddition3(1, 2)          # works
myAddition3(2.5,(2.3+3.9)) # works
myAddition3(3, "4")        # should generate error
myAddition3((3==5),2)      # is this an error?

                       What is the printout?         CODE
 return Statement                                                            50


• return causes function to exit and return a value to where the function
  was called
• The return value may any data type


def functionName(parameters):
    # code that does the work...
    return expression #optional
 return Statement Example   51




def add(a, b):
    return a + b

x = 10
y = 5
total = add(x, y)
print("sum = ", total)
                                                                  52
Functions may return a value
def add(x,y):
     try:
            return x + y
     except:
            print("Error: function add expects two numbers")



print(add(5.3, 4.7))
print(add(5.3, "4.7"))
print(add("5.3","4.7")) # does this behave as expected?

                                                          CODE
                                                                    53
Function Effects #1: Do something + Print

# this function simply prints the elements in companyList
def printCompanies(companyNames):
      for c in companyNames:
            print(c)

companyList = ['Apple', 'nVidia', 'Microsoft', 'Meta']
printCompanies(companyList)




                                                            CODE
                                                                         54
Function Effects #2: Calculate + Return Value
# this function returns the result of its calculation
def average(*args): # *args allows for any number of arguments
     sum = 0
     for x in args:
            sum = sum + x
     return sum / len(args)

Need to use or save result or it is lost

average(39, 68, 78, 89) # data lost
classMedian = average(39, 68, 78, 89)
print(classMedian)
averagePassing = average(39, 68, 78, 89) >= 65
print(averagePassing)


                                                                 CODE
                                                                                                55
Function Effects #3: Alter + Return a Copy

Same as #3

Note: Methods for immutable (unchangeable) data types (strings, dictionaries) do this



fruit = 'apple'                                        fruit = 'apple'
fruit.upper()                                          fruit = fruit.upper()
print(fruit)                                           print(fruit)
                              Result lost




                                                                                        CODE
                                                                               56
Function Effects #4: Alter Directly
Often done by methods (functions) of mutable (changeable) data types
We will not write functions that do this.


def printCompanies(companyNames):
      for c in companyNames:
            print(c)
      return

companyList = ['Zillow', 'nVidia', 'Microsoft', 'Meta', 'Apple']
printCompanies(companyList)
print()
companyList.sort()    #alter the list
printCompanies(companyList)
                                                                       CODE
                                                                          57
Scope of a Variable
• The scope of a variable is the part of that code where that variable
 is accessible

• Local variable
   ⎻ either declared within the function or is a parameter in the
     definition of the function
   ⎻ accessible within the function

• Global variable
   ⎻ declared outside of function definitions
   ⎻ accessible from anywhere within the program
                                                                       58
Example: Global vs. Local Variable
artist = "Leonardo Da Vinci" #artist is a global variable

def printArtistName(artist): #is this 'artist' a global variable?
    internalVar1 = artist #internalVar1 is a local variable
    print(artist, "is an artist")

printArtistName(artist)
printArtistName("Zeng Fanzhi")

# what if we run the line below?
printArtistName(internalVar1)




                                                               CODE
                                                                      59
Recap Exercise
x = 10
if x == 5:
    print('x is equal to 5')
    print('print out only if x is equal to 5')
print('anything')
if x > 5:
    print('x is greater than 5')
if x < 10:
    print('x is less than 10')
if x <= 10:
    print('x is less than or equal to 10')
                                      What is the printout?
                                                              CODE
                                                          60
Recap Exercise
a = 5
b = a ** 0

if b < 2:      #Option1
    c = 1
elif b == 1:   #Option2
    c = 2
else:          #Option3
    c = 3
print(a + b + c)

                          What is the printout?
                                                  CODE
                                                                             61
Recap Exercise
• Build a list with the following four elements
      1, hello, [1,2,3,4], and True


• Use a loop to print out each element in the list
myList = [1, 'hello', [1,2,3,4], True]
for i in myList:
    print(i)
                                             What is the printout?
                                                                     CODE
                                                                    62
Recap Exercise
print("let's start...")
i = 1
total = 0
while True:
    total += i #total = total + i
    i += 1 #i = i + 1
print ('Sum of 1 through ---- is ', total)




                                    What is the printout?
                                                            CODE
 Recap Exercise                                                    63


• Create a function subtract() which accepts arguments a
 and b and returns the result a – b
     def subtract(a, b):
         try:
              return a - b
         except:
              print("subtract expects two numbers")

     x = 10
     y = 5
     differ = subtract(x, y)
     print('difference =', differ)

                                                           CODE
                                                     65
Benefits of Functions
• Eliminate repetitive code
• Make complex code easier to read and understand
• Makes debugging easier
• Allows reuse of code in other programs
                                                                       66
input() Function
• Allows program to get keyboard
• Optional argument: user prompt (string)
• During execution, program stops and waits for user to type, press
  Enter
• Function returns the value that was typed by user as a string
• This can be stored in a variable for further processing
userInput = int(input("Enter a number: "))
print(userInput)
 User Input Validation with while Loop                                    67

and try… except…
#how to get a positive integer input using a while loop
while True:
    try:
         userInput = int(input("Enter a number: "))
         if userInput > 0:
             break
         print("input int is not positive")
    except:
         print("invalid input")
print(userInput)

                    Question: Can this be a function for future reuse?
                                                                       68
“Guess the Number” Game
• Python picks a random number within a range
• Give the user some number of tries to guess the number
• After each guess, tell the user whether the guess is too high or
 too low
• User wins if the number is guessed within guess limit, and
 loses otherwise



                                                               CODE
             69
Questions
